sentences.text: About 20 kilometer.
No, what I say?
No, less, less, about 10 kilometer.
Yes.
Yes, I did.
When was that?
It was right away when I went-- I-- I came to Olecko.
The-- the [INAUDIBLE], the Goy, which I was hiding there, you wanted-- I'll tell you how I was liberated.
I was liberated by [INAUDIBLE].
And when I saw the soldiers, they are Russian.
But we were hiding.
Then, one saw us hiding, me and Sanja.
Then, they told-- Sanja what?
Sanja Feldman.
He say, what is the two-- two girls are doing here?
He say, this is my sister's girls.
They are coming here, because the Polish burning their village.
He said, no, this is not your sisters.
You tell me better the truth.
Yes, the Russian.
Then, he was afraid.
He say, OK.
You must to-- to tell.
Then, I-- I-- I told the lady-- he-- he came to me.
This was [INAUDIBLE].
He say, look, I want to know.
Because now is war.
And you must, because children are the-- the best spies.
I say, no.
I cannot tell you here.
Then, you'll tell-- he said, what?
The [INAUDIBLE] used to shoot the Russian Army.
I say, no.
If you'll take me in a-- in a separate room, I'll tell you.
He took me in separate room and said, who you are?
I say, I am a Jewish girl.
I was here hiding.
I start to cry like a baby.
And he said, my God, I don't believe in God, but I was sending-- you've been through so much.
This-- no-- Suffering.
Suffering.
And I needed to speak so-- to you.
And you know, and he came out and he told-- I know that I said, but I said, don't tell nobody because you'll go and they'll burn all the family.
There was a family about 12 people.
Then he-- he-- they say, no, no, no.
He say, I still need to-- some questions to ask.
And then-- and then they took him-- the Goy-- to-- to burn the-- the-- the-- the-- the dead people.
And then he saw our Jewish-- he didn't know what to do with us.
The-- the-- the--
No, they were guarding almost.
They were guarding.
And he knew he was afraid they'll kill us.
Then, he-- he-- he went to-- to the-- to work, and there, he saw this officer-- a Jewish-- a Jewish nose.
He said, you are Jewish?
He say, yes.
You were crying to so-and-so.
You want maybe to kill me too?
He say, no, no, no.
I have two girls which I hided.
And on that morning, somebody saw us.
And I say, Sanja, I don't know.
I-- I shouldn't go.
I shouldn't go.
We should be here.
I don't know why we should be here.
And he said, look, I'll go with you but in case, if they are not there, I'll kill you.
He took two soldiers with him.
I'll kill you with whole family, because you are liar.
He came, you know, and it was good that we didn't go.
If we would go, he would kill the whole family.
And he say-- he say, go out, people.
Go out.
And he start to-- he was from Kiev.
He start to cry.
And he told [INAUDIBLE].
And he gave him the two good horses, and he took the bad for the soldiers.
And he said, no more.
He gave him no more to the-- to the war, to the front.
And he say, now, you-- you need to do something.
Go right away and take them to-- right away and take them to-- to-- to our city.
He took us to Olecko.
To Olecko, where my sister and brother.
This was the nearest.
He took to Olecko.
They-- they saw they were crying.
Oh, my god.
You brought, [NON-ENGLISH].
He said, no, no.
I don't leave us-- you here.
Then he brought us to the-- to the road of Rowne.
It was-- Rowne was big bombardment, big-- the Germans.
And they say the-- the-- the-- the soldiers, when they go, where are you going?
They bomb-- they bombadier the Rowne.
Where are you going?
I said, we go on the market.
And what we'll tell?
We were afraid.
When we came to Rowne, and there, we were liberated.
And we were in Rowne all year, all year from-- When did you find your mother and brother again?
No, the brother was killed.
My brother my brother went to one, which by-- by a forest.
He was a-- a-- a shoe repairman, a old one.
And the poor brother, what do we say?
You know [NON-ENGLISH]?
You want to-- to-- to help me to repair shoes, then you can be.
He say, OK.
He was very OK.
He never-- in our family was no shoes-- shoemaker, nothing.
And he start and he say--
Elia was 27, born '27.
He was born in '27.
He was a 14-year-old, you know?
And-- and-- and then he-- he say, you know the [INAUDIBLE], we could make a pair of shoes.
We say, OK.
Then he took, you know, boat leather and he make a pair of shoes.
He saw how they're made, you know?
He made a pair of shoes.
And then he-- he-- he steal for us, too-- to-- leather and he made for us.
We came barefoot in snow [INAUDIBLE].
He gave me and my-- me and Sanja, two pair of shoes he brought to us, We were in contact that time.
And I say-- I say-- relief-- relief-- This is all-- I say I'm afraid that, you know, that I am there already.
And then-- then they find out who he is, and they killed him.
From [INAUDIBLE] farm was.
They recognize him and they say, oh, this is David's son.
You know who he is?
You know?
And there's-- there, oy, I don't want to know.
And my mother, they told her all the things.
She got distraught, and I took care a year, all year of her.
She died in Rowne.
I buried her.
Yes.
Oh, I forgot this in Rowne.
From the--
What we went to, you know, to find-- to to Israel, the place where they-- where they kill the Jews.
Then we find the shoes, these diapers, all kind of clothes.
This was in-- in '44.
This was like a year or two years of still [INAUDIBLE] we find.
And when we-- when we-- no.
No.
When we start to [INAUDIBLE].
When we start to [SPEAKING GERMAN].
No.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
To dig-- to dig around the-- the-- the grave.
We do that.
We wanted to-- to know that this and this.
We wanted to-- to put on this stone, but we didn't have time.
We left in '45.
In '45, we left already Rowne.
Yes, yes, digging around the grave.
I don't remember, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
I don't remember the name of the-- of the-- was a small-- a small, not a forest, how you say, a [INAUDIBLE].
A park.
A park.
Yes.
We went and we found all kind of [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Some bones also buried there.
I they told me, but I couldn't go there.
I-- as a pauper, after the war-- after the war, I took such revenge of this what killed my father, the ones that took away everything.
How?
They-- I came to my place and there was the Czech was still there.
And I came to the-- to the mayor, you know, and I recognized all the-- the equipment and everything.
I said, oy, this is mine.
He said, oh, no this is not yours.
Go right away from here.
And you know, I-- I took very bad steps.
He could kill me with Sanja, and we ran away.
And I came to the neighbor, a Czech, first, which they neighbored our--
Farms, yes.
And I called-- told her and I-- I cried very much, and she gave me bread and-- and this--
Bacon-- and bacon.
And at night, she was very rich, a nice house-- came the- the-- not [INAUDIBLE], the-- the like-- the-- the-- from the-- from the Army, like in come from the Army.
Yes.
And they made there their-- their headquarter there.
And the soldiers were in the-- in the forest.
There was a big forest where our village was.
And she told the whole story.
They went to him.
They arrested him.
They arrested him, and they brought him in the shtetl and I was there, yes.
And-- and-- and he says come in to Lutsk, you must be a witness and I was a witness.
And I told, I think [INAUDIBLE], I told that he took away everything and he-- he almost killed me, which was-- was-- was true.
And he got death penalty, and then he-- he-- he told who killed, yes.
He told who killed, you know?
He gave out everyone who killed.
And then I came to my shtetl.
They told me that Kowalski, the commandant from the shtetl, the Ukrainian, which, you know, organized to rape and to-- and to kill and to take away, he was there.
They-- they caught him and I was another witness.
For that trial and some others, I was, I was.
I did my-- my job.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
Yes.
No, on there-- in Munich.
I stayed in Munich from '45 to '49.
To Israel.
I go by airplane.
Yes.
I found my brother's brother-in-law in Israel.
No, there was no need to sponsor right away.
I had-- I was nurse in Germany.
Yes.
I-- I finished nursing school in Germany.
And there was a [INAUDIBLE] from Israel, a [INAUDIBLE] which know only Hebrew and he broke a leg, and nobody could understand him.
And they had-- they know that I speak Hebrew.
They took me and I translated.
And he say, what?
We saw Hebrew, you want to go to America?
I'll send you [INAUDIBLE].
What day you want?
And I say-- and I say, OK.
And he influenced on me that I go to Israel.
No, there was no pay.
The Joint helped us out.
Yes, gave us packages and the German-- we had [INAUDIBLE].
One moment.
I'm so [INAUDIBLE]. '25, '49.
A 24-year-old.
I meet a lot.
Someone special?
I-- I meet-- I was engaged to a doctor, but didn't go.
I changed my mind.
And then I met my husband.
He was-- he-- he was a-- a Russian.
He finished MH degree-- MA degree-- and in Israel, he started a Ph degree and he finished in Los Angeles with a Ph degree in UCLA.
Michel Grinstein.
He had a father her.
He was hiding with a father.
Also also by a-- by a Baptist. 12 people-- Pavloski-- 12 people he-- he was hiding.
In Israel.
Yes.
I miscarriaged after and before, and I had one son.
David.
Like my father.
And he was sick and he had cancer on the thyroid.
And he's buried here in Florida.
I was in Los Angeles.
We used to live in Los Angeles.
He went here to college.
He woul be 36.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
To Los Angeles I directly.
He was sick and asthma.
He had very bad asthma.
And I was waiting, waiting, but I couldn't wait anymore because he was so small and he got-- got his [INAUDIBLE] And the second day, he start to be blue.
And I decided to go, and we went here and right away, he was 6'2' He was tall and dark and handsome.
And he went through from the asthma.
And I think, Thank God.
Thank God.
And then this.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
To Florida I moved in '83.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
You know, not to-- not to trust people, not to trust the countries, not to trust, you know, leaders too much.
To remember what happened by Hitler.
It could be repeat again if we wouldn't be careful.
Because in my time now, they say already there couldn't be.
This a lie.
In our time.
Now, I want to say so.
The Polacks, the Ukrainian, the Lithuanian, I don't know but they say, that the-- but the Ukraine and-- and the Polacks I went on my own skin.
They are-- they are-- their-- their conscience is not clear because they are 99% is their fault that we suffered.
We lost so much from our family.
The-- if not there, would be in our-- in our section, from Ukraine would be alive today-- 60% people would be alive.

Because the-- the German came three, four people and the rest was the-- the-- the-- the-- Banderas with the police and everything in the-- in the-- in the forest, where the people, the Jewish people ran out to the forest was killed through the [NON-ENGLISH] and Bandera, the party Bandera.
[PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]
And now, they-- they deny.
And most can write down, now, take sides that is not-- signatures that is not-- not true after the war kills it to take [INAUDIBLE], which went through by miracle alive to be 42 people to kill?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
And you know, you know, I feel shameful-- shameful, you know.
This is also, he told me the story.
He was alive, he, his son, and his father and a brother.
There was three.
You know, they came in the shtetl by-- by the-- and it was a small shtetl.
And he went to see what-- with the brother what they can-- where to go.
And the-- the son said [SPEAKING HEBREW] take me with you.
Take me with you.
He said, no, no.
Be with the Zayde.
He went with the Zayde.
They come.
They kill the Zayde, killed him.
[INAUDIBLE]
In-- in a shtetl in Poland.
No, he's alive.
He's here.
He told me the story.
